subtopic_detail: Catatonia diagnosis and lorazepam challenge in elderly inpatient
stem:
A 76-year-old man with a 5-year history of severe recurrent major depression is admitted to the geriatric psychiatry unit after a 2-week deterioration. He has stopped eating and drinking, says nothing in response to questions, and remains motionless in bed. The nursing staff describe "waxy flexibility" when his arm is moved, and he has held an unusual posture for over 30 minutes during the morning. He has not been febrile but his urine output has dropped over the last 24 hours. His usual medications include sertraline 100 mg daily and zopiclone 7.5 mg nocte. He has no recent dose changes and no infection.

The geriatric psychiatry consultant has examined him and the on-call medical registrar has reviewed his blood results.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 102/68 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 98% in room air, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Mute, motionless, waxy flexibility on passive limb movement, prolonged posturing (>30 minutes), no rigidity beyond catalepsy, no tremor, no clonus, no fever
data_table.Investigations: FBC within normal limits, UEC creatinine 132 micromol/L (60-110, baseline 88), CK 1240 U/L (<200), lactate 1.4, CRP 4 mg/L (<5), MRI brain unchanged from 6 months ago, EEG generalised slowing without status epilepticus
data_table.Medications: Sertraline 100 mg daily, zopiclone 7.5 mg nocte

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase sertraline to 200 mg orally daily for depressive psychomotor retardation
options.rationale: Increasing SSRI dose does not treat catatonia and may delay the diagnostic test. Catatonia is a syndrome that requires its own treatment first, even when underlying depression is the cause. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Refer to neurology for autoimmune encephalitis workup before treatment
options.rationale: Autoimmune encephalitis is a differential but the clinical features (catalepsy, waxy flexibility, mutism, in a known severe MDD patient) point first to catatonia; lorazepam challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic and should not be delayed. Autoimmune workup proceeds in parallel. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous lorazepam 2 mg as a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge
options.rationale: Catatonia is diagnosed using the Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale (catalepsy, waxy flexibility, mutism, immobility, posturing, stereotypy, and others). A lorazepam challenge of 2 mg IV (or 4 mg) within 10 to 20 minutes is both diagnostic (response within 10 minutes) and the first-line treatment. If no response, ECT is the definitive treatment. CK elevation and rising creatinine here reflect immobility-related rhabdomyolysis and AKI - these add urgency. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Commence olanzapine 10 mg orally daily for psychomotor agitation
options.rationale: Antipsychotics may precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) in catatonia or worsen catatonic features; they are contraindicated in pure catatonia until catatonia has resolved with benzodiazepines or ECT. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Commence intravenous fluids, monitor input and output, and review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Supportive care addresses the AKI but does not treat the catatonia. Delaying lorazepam challenge by 24 hours risks worsening rhabdomyolysis and AKI. (Source: RANZCP Practice Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is recognised by mutism, immobility, catalepsy, waxy flexibility and posturing. Lorazepam 2 mg IV is both diagnostic (response within 10 to 20 minutes) and first-line treatment. ECT is the definitive treatment if no response or if life-threatening catatonia (malignant catatonia: fever, autonomic instability, rhabdomyolysis). Antipsychotics are contraindicated until catatonia resolves because of NMS risk.
key_points: Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale: ≥2 features for diagnosis
Lorazepam 2 mg IV challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic (response in 10-20 minutes)
Antipsychotics contraindicated until catatonia resolves because of NMS risk
explanation.pearls: Malignant catatonia has hyperthermia, autonomic instability and rhabdomyolysis, mimicking NMS, and is a medical emergency treated with ECT. Always check CK, creatinine, and consider DVT prophylaxis given prolonged immobility.

subtopic_detail: Lithium toxicity at therapeutic level with NSAID interaction
stem:
A 45-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder on lithium 750 mg orally daily for 8 years is reviewed for new confusion, tremor and ataxia over the past 5 days. Her serum lithium concentration today is 0.85 mmol/L (target 0.6 to 1.0 mmol/L for maintenance). She has been taking ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily for the past 10 days for shoulder pain prescribed by her GP. She also takes perindopril 5 mg daily for hypertension that was started 6 months ago.

Her last clozapine review documented stable mood for 3 years. She has no recent fever, vomiting or diarrhoea.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88, BP 132/82 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 98% in room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Mildly drowsy, oriented to person, coarse postural tremor, broad-based gait with truncal ataxia, hyperreflexia, no clonus
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 0.85 mmol/L (0.6-1.0), creatinine 142 micromol/L (60-110, baseline 88), Na 138 mmol/L (135-145), TSH 4.8 mIU/L (0.4-4.0), CRP 6 mg/L (<5), urine output 30 mL/hour over 6 hours, ECG normal QT
data_table.Medications: Lithium 750 mg daily, perindopril 5 mg daily, ibuprofen 400 mg TDS (10 days)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Switch lithium to sodium valproate 500 mg orally twice daily
options.rationale: Switching mood stabiliser while neurotoxic worsens the picture; first address toxicity. Mood-stabiliser substitution during acute toxicity also raises the risk of mood relapse before steady state is achieved. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook)

options.text: Refer for emergency haemodialysis to remove lithium urgently
options.rationale: Haemodialysis is indicated for severe lithium toxicity: lithium above 4.0 mmol/L, persistent altered consciousness, AKI not responding to fluids, or seizures. She has a borderline-therapeutic level with renal impairment that may respond to fluid resuscitation; HD is excessive at this point. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook)

options.text: Continue current lithium dose and reassess in clinic in 1 week
options.rationale: Continuing lithium in the face of new neurological signs and rising creatinine risks progression to severe toxicity. Even with serum level in therapeutic range, CNS lithium accumulates over days in chronic toxicity. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook)

options.text: Restrict free water and add furosemide for renal recovery support
options.rationale: Furosemide increases lithium reabsorption in the proximal tubule by causing volume depletion; it can worsen lithium toxicity. Restricting free water is also counterproductive in dehydration-induced lithium toxicity. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook)

options.text: Stop ibuprofen and perindopril, hold lithium, give IV fluids, monitor serially
options.rationale: Chronic lithium toxicity can occur at therapeutic-range levels when renal clearance falls due to NSAID and ACE-I inhibition of renal prostaglandin and afferent arteriolar tone. Neurological features (confusion, tremor, ataxia, hyperreflexia) without GI symptoms are the textbook signature. Treatment is to stop precipitants, restore intravascular volume, and watch renal recovery; haemodialysis is reserved for severe toxicity (lithium >4.0, GCS impaired, AKI not responding, seizures). (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook)

explanation.summary: Chronic lithium toxicity at therapeutic serum levels occurs when renal clearance falls due to NSAIDs, ACE-I, thiazides, dehydration, or new renal impairment. CNS features (confusion, tremor, ataxia, hyperreflexia, dysarthria) dominate over GI symptoms. Treat by stopping precipitants and restoring intravascular volume. Haemodialysis for severe toxicity.
key_points: NSAIDs and ACE-I are the two most common drug precipitants of chronic lithium toxicity
Haemodialysis indicated for lithium >4.0 mmol/L or severe neurotoxicity
Lithium has its own elimination half-life independent of dialysis; rebound after HD is common
explanation.pearls: Long-term lithium monitoring requires 6-monthly lithium levels, 12-monthly UEC, TSH (lithium-induced hypothyroidism), and PTH/calcium (lithium-induced hyperparathyroidism). Educate patients to hold lithium during gastroenteritis and to avoid OTC NSAIDs.

subtopic_detail: Postpartum psychosis treatment escalation - ECT vs lithium
stem:
A 31-year-old woman is admitted to a perinatal mother-baby psychiatric unit 6 days postpartum following her first delivery. She has had 48 hours of severe insomnia, fluctuant mood between elation and confusion, paranoid persecutory delusions about strangers harming her baby, and command auditory hallucinations telling her to "get rid of" the baby. She is breastfeeding. The on-call perinatal psychiatrist has assessed her with the on-call consultant. She has a maternal aunt with bipolar disorder. The baby is being co-cared with her in the mother-baby unit with continuous staff oversight.

She has been started on olanzapine 10 mg orally nocte 48 hours ago with limited response and her delusions have intensified overnight.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 110, BP 134/82 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 98% in room air, temperature 37.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Disorganised speech, fluctuant mood, persistent delusions and auditory hallucinations, no fever, no focal neurology, no thyroid enlargement
data_table.Investigations: FBC within normal limits, UEC within normal limits, TSH within normal limits, B12 within normal limits, MRI brain not yet performed
data_table.Medications: Olanzapine 10 mg nocte (commenced 48 hours ago); paracetamol prn

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase olanzapine to 20 mg nocte and observe for further 72 hours
options.rationale: Dose escalation may eventually help but the time-critical risk to mother and baby makes rapid response necessary. ECT achieves remission in days versus weeks. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health

options.text: Commence lithium 250 mg orally twice daily and discontinue breastfeeding
options.rationale: Lithium is the long-term prophylaxis of choice (recurrence risk above 50% in subsequent pregnancies) but its onset for acute psychosis is slower than ECT. Breastfeeding can continue under specialist supervision with infant monitoring. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health

options.text: Add diazepam 10 mg orally three times daily for symptomatic relief
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines may sedate but do not treat the underlying psychosis; high doses risk respiratory depression and accumulate in breast milk. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health

options.text: Refer to community mental health team for outpatient follow-up
options.rationale: Outpatient management is not appropriate for a psychotic mother with command hallucinations to harm her baby. Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health

options.text: Refer for urgent electroconvulsive therapy with continued co-care of the baby
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis with command hallucinations to harm the baby and limited response to antipsychotic at 48 hours is a psychiatric emergency. RANZCP guidance supports ECT as the rapid-onset definitive treatment with very high response rates (over 80%) in postpartum psychosis, especially when there is harm risk to mother or baby and antipsychotic response is limited. Lithium is the maintenance choice, but ECT achieves remission faster than dose escalation. Mother-baby care continues with continuous staff oversight. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health

explanation.summary: Postpartum psychosis (incidence 1 to 2 per 1000) presents in the first 2 weeks postpartum with mood elevation, paranoid delusions, command hallucinations and severe insomnia. It is a psychiatric emergency. AU first-line is high-potency antipsychotic; ECT is preferred for rapid response in severe cases with harm risk. Long-term prophylaxis with lithium is offered because of high recurrence (over 50%) in subsequent pregnancies.
key_points: ECT achieves remission in days for severe postpartum psychosis with command hallucinations
Recurrence in subsequent pregnancies is above 50% - long-term lithium prophylaxis is offered
Mother-baby separation is not mandatory; continuous staff oversight in MBU is the AU model
explanation.pearls: Untreated mortality risk from infanticide (4%) and suicide (5%) makes urgent treatment time-critical. Future pregnancies require pre-pregnancy psychiatric planning with prophylactic lithium often re-introduced in the third trimester or immediately postpartum.

sources.label: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline: Perinatal Mental Health

stem:
A 64-year-old woman presents to a metropolitan ED at 0900 with central chest tightness that started at 0700 and has now resolved. She has type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and a 30 pack-year smoking history. Her father had an MI at 55. Initial ECG shows normal sinus rhythm with no ST changes or T-wave inversion.

Her first hs-troponin is back, and a second draw at one hour is now available.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 138/82 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 98% in room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Comfortable, no respiratory distress, chest clear, normal heart sounds with no murmurs, no peripheral oedema, calves soft
data_table.Investigations: ECG: normal sinus rhythm, no ST changes; hs-troponin T at 0 hours 8 ng/L (sex-specific 99th percentile 14 ng/L), 1-hour hs-troponin T 9 ng/L; renal function within normal limits; chest X-ray clear
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1 g BD, perindopril 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nocte; no aspirin

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for serial troponin testing every 6 hours for 24 hours
options.rationale: 6-hourly serial troponin protocols precede hs-troponin and are no longer the standard. CSANZ accepts the 0/1h hs-troponin algorithm for rule-out, freeing low-risk patients. (Source: Heart Foundation / CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

options.text: Commence dual antiplatelet therapy and admit to coronary care
options.rationale: DAPT for ACS rule-in is not indicated when troponin trajectory rules out NSTEMI; bleeding risk without benefit. (Source: Heart Foundation / CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

options.text: Discharge home for outpatient cardiology with cardiac stress test
options.rationale: The European Society of Cardiology 0/1h hs-troponin T rule-out algorithm (also accepted in AU CSANZ pathways) considers a 0-hour value <5 ng/L OR 0-hour <12 with 1-hour delta <3 ng/L as a rule-out for NSTEMI. Her 0-hour 8 with 1-hour delta of 1 ng/L meets rule-out criteria. Symptoms have resolved and ECG is non-diagnostic so outpatient cardiology review with stress test is appropriate. (Source: Heart Foundation / CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

options.text: Activate the cath lab for urgent coronary angiography now
options.rationale: Urgent coronary angiography is for STEMI, NSTEMI rule-in with high-risk features (haemodynamic instability, dynamic ECG changes, persistent angina), or unstable angina. She has rule-out troponin trajectory and no high-risk features. (Source: Heart Foundation / CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

options.text: Order a CT coronary angiogram before discharge planning
options.rationale: CT coronary angiogram is useful in intermediate-risk patients to refine the diagnosis but is not the standard immediate step after rule-out by 0/1h algorithm; stress testing is preferred for functional assessment in outpatient cardiology pathways. (Source: Heart Foundation / CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

explanation.summary: The CSANZ-endorsed 0/1h hs-troponin algorithm allows rule-out of NSTEMI when 0-hour hs-troponin <5 ng/L OR 0-hour <12 with 1-hour delta <3 ng/L, with non-diagnostic ECG and resolved symptoms. Rule-in is 0-hour ≥52 ng/L OR 1-hour delta ≥5 ng/L. Intermediate values need observation and 3-hour repeat.
key_points: 0/1h hs-troponin algorithm: rule-out <5 at 0h or <12 with delta <3 at 1h
Rule-in: 0h ≥52 ng/L OR 1h delta ≥5 ng/L
Outpatient cardiology stress test or CT coronary angiogram is the post-rule-out pathway
explanation.pearls: The 0/1h algorithm requires assay-specific cut-offs (different for hs-troponin T vs hs-troponin I). Sex-specific 99th percentile cut-offs apply for the rule-in side. Use clinical judgement in patients with renal impairment - the baseline hs-troponin is often above 14 ng/L without acute MI.

subtopic_detail: Community-acquired pneumonia SMART-COP and ICU admission
stem:
A 68-year-old man with COPD on tiotropium presents with a 3-day history of productive cough, fever, and rigors. He is admitted from ED to a general medical ward. Six hours later he becomes more dyspnoeic and the registrar is called.

He has not had any antibiotics in the last 6 months.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124, BP 86/52 mmHg, RR 36, SpO2 88% on 6 L/min via Hudson mask, temperature 38.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Confused (AMTS 4/10), accessory muscle use, right-sided crackles, no wheeze, well-perfused but cool peripheries
data_table.Investigations: Chest X-ray: dense right lower lobe consolidation with small parapneumonic effusion. ABG on 6 L/min: pH 7.32, pO2 56 mmHg, pCO2 38 mmHg, HCO3 19 mmol/L; lactate 4.2; WCC 22 x10^9/L; albumin 26 g/L; sodium 132 mmol/L; urea 10.2 mmol/L; creatinine 102 micromol/L; CRP 318 mg/L
data_table.Medications: Tiotropium 18 micrograms daily; ED ceftriaxone 2 g + azithromycin 500 mg PO given on arrival 6 hours ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Bridge to non-invasive ventilation with BiPAP and reassess in 2 hours
options.rationale: BiPAP is appropriate for COPD-related hypercapnic respiratory failure but he is currently hypoxic and septic with confusion; BiPAP risks aspiration in the obtunded patient and delays intubation. (Source: TSANZ / Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory CAP)

options.text: Continue ceftriaxone monotherapy on the ward with 2 hourly review
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone monotherapy is appropriate empirically only for mild-to-moderate CAP; with SMART-COP 11 and septic shock the patient needs ICU-level care and broader empirical cover (eg piperacillin-tazobactam + azithromycin or as per local antibiogram). (Source: TSANZ / Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory CAP)

options.text: Insert intercostal drain for the small parapneumonic effusion
options.rationale: Small simple parapneumonic effusions are managed with antibiotics; drainage is reserved for empyema or large effusion compromising oxygenation. The dominant issue is sepsis and respiratory failure, not the effusion. (Source: TSANZ / Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory CAP)

options.text: Calculate SMART-COP and refer to ICU for likely intubation and inotropic support
options.rationale: SMART-COP: low Systolic BP (2), Multilobar / large effusion (1), low Albumin (1), high Respiratory rate (1), Tachycardia (1), Confusion (1), low Oxygen (2), low pH (2) = 11. Above 5 predicts >65% need for ventilatory or inotropic support and mandates ICU referral. The AU TSANZ pathway uses SMART-COP for site-of-care decisions in addition to severity-of-illness scoring like APACHE. (Source: TSANZ / Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory CAP)

options.text: Discharge with oral amoxicillin-clavulanate after 24-hour observation
options.rationale: Discharge with oral antibiotics is not an option in septic shock with multilobar pneumonia and respiratory failure. (Source: TSANZ / Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory CAP)

explanation.summary: SMART-COP scores Systolic BP, Multilobar, Albumin, Respiratory rate, Tachycardia, Confusion, Oxygen, pH, and predicts need for intensive respiratory or vasopressor support (IRVS). Above 5 has over 50% positive predictive value for IRVS. AU TSANZ guidelines incorporate this for site-of-care decisions.
key_points: SMART-COP ≥5 predicts ICU need; ≥3 is the typical hospital-admission threshold
Severe CAP requires broad cover: piperacillin-tazobactam + azithromycin or per local antibiogram
BiPAP is contraindicated in obtunded patients with aspiration risk
explanation.pearls: AU TSANZ guidelines emphasise that PSI and CURB-65 underestimate severity in younger patients without comorbidity. SMART-COP is calibrated for IRVS prediction. Add gentamicin for atypicals coverage and Pseudomonas in selected patients.

stem:
A 58-year-old woman with no provoking risk factors presents with mild right-sided chest discomfort. CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) reports a single subsegmental pulmonary embolus in the right lower lobe with no proximal involvement. Bilateral leg ultrasound is negative for DVT. She has no haemodynamic compromise, no right heart strain on echocardiogram, and a normal troponin. She has no cancer, no recent surgery, no immobility, and no oestrogen exposure.

The respiratory and vascular teams have reviewed the imaging and discussed treatment options with her.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 124/78 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 98% in room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Comfortable, no respiratory distress, chest clear, calves soft and non-tender, no chest wall tenderness
data_table.Investigations: CTPA: solitary subsegmental PE in right lower lobe, no main pulmonary artery / lobar / segmental PE; lower limb Doppler bilateral negative; D-dimer raised; troponin within normal limits; echocardiogram normal RV function; FBC unremarkable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence rivaroxaban 15 mg BD for 21 days then 20 mg daily indefinitely
options.rationale: Indefinite (extended) anticoagulation is reserved for unprovoked proximal PE with low bleeding risk or recurrent VTE. A single subsegmental PE without confirmed VTE elsewhere does not yet warrant indefinite therapy; the 3-month course is the standard initial period. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Commence warfarin with INR target 2 to 3 for at least 6 months
options.rationale: Warfarin is no longer first-line for VTE in AU practice; DOACs are preferred because of fewer monitoring requirements and similar efficacy. Warfarin remains the choice in mechanical valves, antiphospholipid syndrome, and severe renal impairment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Withhold anticoagulation with surveillance lower limb Doppler weekly for 2 weeks
options.rationale: Surveillance without anticoagulation is a CHEST guideline option for subsegmental PE with confirmed absence of proximal DVT, low recurrence risk, and patient preference for not taking anticoagulants; however the AU consensus still favours treating because diagnostic accuracy of subsegmental PE on CTPA is limited and elevated D-dimer here suggests ongoing clot. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Commence apixaban 10 mg BD for 7 days then 5 mg BD for 3 months total
options.rationale: Single subsegmental PE with no proximal DVT, no risk factors, low cancer risk, and no haemodynamic compromise represents an isolated low-risk PE. AU practice supports a 3-month course of anticoagulation as the standard duration for low-risk PE. Apixaban 10 mg BD for 7 days then 5 mg BD is the AU first-line DOAC regimen for VTE; surveillance proximal vein Doppler is an alternative only in selected cases. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Treat with LMWH for 5 days then warfarin for 3 months at INR 2 to 3
options.rationale: LMWH bridging to warfarin is the alternative but DOACs do not require bridging and are preferred for non-cancer-associated VTE in AU. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: Subsegmental PE without proximal DVT and without risk factors can be considered for surveillance-only management per CHEST guidelines, but AU practice typically still anticoagulates for 3 months because of false-negative scan rates and elevated D-dimer indicating ongoing clot. Apixaban 10 mg BD for 7 days then 5 mg BD is the first-line DOAC for VTE.
key_points: Apixaban 10 mg BD for 7 days, then 5 mg BD - AU first-line for VTE
Rivaroxaban 15 mg BD for 21 days, then 20 mg daily - alternative DOAC for VTE
Subsegmental PE surveillance is an alternative only in selected patients with confirmed absence of proximal DVT
explanation.pearls: Indefinite anticoagulation is offered for unprovoked proximal PE with low bleeding risk, recurrent VTE, or cancer-associated VTE. LMWH remains first-line for cancer-associated thrombosis in many AU centres, though apixaban and edoxaban now have growing evidence (eviQ guidelines).

subtopic_detail: Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes - sequence of management
stem:
A 72-year-old man on hospital ward day 2 of admission for community-acquired pneumonia has a routine 8 am UEC return abnormal. He has chronic kidney disease (eGFR 32) and is on perindopril 10 mg daily and spironolactone 25 mg daily for heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. He feels generally unwell with mild lethargy.

The medical registrar is at the bedside reviewing the ECG.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 58, BP 132/78 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 96% in room air, temperature 37.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Mild lethargy, no proximal weakness, no chest pain
data_table.Investigations: K 6.8 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), creatinine 188 micromol/L (60-110, baseline 156), Na 138 mmol/L, HCO3 18 mmol/L, urea 18 mmol/L; ECG: PR 220 ms, peaked T waves in V2-V5, no QRS widening, no LBBB
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 10 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily; ceftriaxone for pneumonia

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Give 50 mL of 50% dextrose intravenously without insulin cover
options.rationale: Dextrose without insulin does not effectively shift potassium intracellularly. Insulin is the active agent (10 units short-acting IV with 50 mL of 50% dextrose to avoid hypoglycaemia). (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Refer for urgent haemodialysis without further pharmacological measures
options.rationale: Haemodialysis is the definitive treatment for refractory hyperkalaemia but takes time to organise; immediate calcium gluconate plus insulin/dextrose buys that time. Skipping membrane stabilisation risks fatal arrhythmia during set-up. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Give 10 mL of 10% calcium gluconate intravenously over 5 minutes
options.rationale: Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes (peaked T waves, prolonged PR) is an emergency. The sequence is: stabilise membrane (calcium gluconate 10 mL 10% IV over 5-10 minutes), shift potassium into cells (insulin/dextrose, salbutamol nebs), then remove potassium (resonium, dialysis if refractory). Calcium gluconate is first because the time-critical risk is arrhythmia and cardiac arrest. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Stop perindopril and spironolactone and recheck potassium in 6 hours
options.rationale: Stopping the precipitants is part of management but does not address the immediate ECG-changing hyperkalaemia. Both should be held but acute treatment (calcium then insulin) is the priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Commence resonium 15 g orally four times daily for potassium removal
options.rationale: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (resonium) is for chronic management or as a last-step potassium-remover; it does not address the immediate cardiac membrane risk and has a slow onset. Calcium gluconate must come first. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes is an emergency. Sequence: stabilise the cardiac membrane (calcium gluconate 10 mL 10% IV over 5 to 10 minutes; lasts 30 to 60 minutes); shift K into cells (10 units actrapid in 50 mL 50% dextrose, salbutamol nebs); remove K (resonium, dialysis if refractory). Stop precipitants (ACE-I, MRA, NSAIDs, K-sparing diuretics).
key_points: Calcium gluconate stabilises the cardiac membrane but does not lower potassium - bridge to definitive measures
Insulin (10 units short-acting) plus 50 mL 50% dextrose shifts potassium into cells within 15-30 minutes
Dialysis is the definitive treatment for refractory hyperkalaemia (>6.5 with ECG changes despite medical therapy)
explanation.pearls: Calcium chloride is preferred over calcium gluconate via central access (more elemental calcium per volume). Salbutamol nebulisers (10 to 20 mg) add a second shifting mechanism. Bicarbonate is reserved for acidotic patients. Always recheck potassium 1-2 hours after intervention.

stem:
A 38-year-old man with known epilepsy on lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily is brought to ED having had a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure that has been ongoing for 25 minutes. Paramedics gave intramuscular midazolam 10 mg en route (10 minutes ago), with no cessation of seizure. He has not had a seizure in 6 months and his last lamotrigine level was therapeutic.

Intravenous access has been obtained on arrival, and he is being managed in the resuscitation bay with continuous monitoring.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 130, BP 152/96 mmHg, RR 24, SpO2 92% on 8 L/min via Hudson mask, temperature 37.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Generalised tonic-clonic activity ongoing, foaming at mouth, no head trauma, pupils midpoint and reactive, no focal lateralising signs
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.22 lactate 5.8 glucose 6.4 mmol/L; UEC and FBC pending; lamotrigine level pending
data_table.Medications: Lamotrigine 200 mg BD; IM midazolam 10 mg given 10 minutes ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Give intravenous levetiracetam 60 mg/kg over 10 minutes
options.rationale: Status epilepticus refractory to first-line benzodiazepines requires a second-line non-benzodiazepine anticonvulsant. The AU TSANZ / ANZAN consensus (post-ESETT trial) is that levetiracetam, valproate and fosphenytoin/phenytoin all show similar response rates (~50%); levetiracetam at 60 mg/kg is often first choice because of fewer interactions, no enzyme induction, and tolerability. He should still get a third line agent if seizure continues past this. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Wait 15 minutes to observe for spontaneous seizure cessation
options.rationale: Status epilepticus is defined as seizure activity lasting more than 5 minutes; waiting risks worsening neurological injury (proportional to duration) and progression to refractory status. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Commence intravenous fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg phenytoin equivalents
options.rationale: Fosphenytoin/phenytoin is an acceptable second-line per ESETT and AU TSANZ; either levetiracetam, valproate or phenytoin are reasonable choices. Phenytoin requires cardiac monitoring and is contraindicated in heart block, hypotension, and porphyria; levetiracetam is often preferred. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Repeat intravenous midazolam 10 mg and reassess after 5 minutes
options.rationale: Repeat benzodiazepine dose is appropriate at 5 to 10 minutes if first dose has not worked, but past the 20 to 30 minute mark a second-line non-benzodiazepine is added because of receptor internalisation reducing further benzodiazepine response. Excessive benzodiazepines also cause respiratory depression. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Proceed straight to rapid sequence intubation with thiopentone infusion
options.rationale: Thiopentone or propofol infusions are third-line for refractory status epilepticus after second-line non-benzodiazepine has failed. They require intubation and ICU. Skipping second-line escalates aggressively without an evidence basis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Status epilepticus is seizure activity lasting more than 5 minutes. First-line: IV/IM/buccal benzodiazepine (lorazepam 4 mg IV or midazolam 10 mg IM). Second-line at 20 minutes: levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV, valproate 40 mg/kg IV, or phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV (all similar efficacy per ESETT). Third-line: thiopentone or propofol infusion in ICU.
key_points: Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV is AU first-line second-line agent because of safety and few interactions
ESETT trial: levetiracetam, fosphenytoin and valproate equally effective (~50% response rate)
Refractory status epilepticus requires intubation and continuous EEG monitoring in ICU
explanation.pearls: Always check glucose, Na, Mg, Ca, and ammonia (valproate users) in status. Pyridoxine 100 mg IV is added if INH overdose is suspected. Driving cessation rules: minimum 6 months seizure-free in AU under Austroads.

subtopic_detail: Aortic dissection imaging choice with renal impairment
stem:
A 58-year-old man presents with sudden onset tearing inter-scapular chest pain radiating to the back. He has a history of hypertension on irbesartan 300 mg daily, polycystic kidney disease with baseline eGFR 28, and bicuspid aortic valve. He is haemodynamically stable but anxious. He has a 3 cm aneurysm of the ascending aorta on a previous echocardiogram 12 months ago.

The ED registrar has reviewed him and the on-call cardiothoracic surgical team has been contacted. Imaging is being planned.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 112, BP 188/108 mmHg right arm / 142/86 mmHg left arm, RR 22, SpO2 97% in room air, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Distressed with pain, blood pressure differential right arm vs left arm 46 mmHg systolic, diastolic murmur at right upper sternal border, no neurology, calves soft, equal pulses bilaterally on radial and femoral
data_table.Investigations: Bedside transthoracic echocardiogram shows intimal flap visible in ascending aorta and mild aortic regurgitation; creatinine 220 micromol/L (60-110, baseline 188); chest X-ray widened mediastinum; troponin within normal limits; ECG normal sinus rhythm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next imaging step?

options.text: Proceed to MRI angiography of the thoracic and abdominal aorta urgently
options.rationale: MRI is contraindicated by time delay in acute Stanford A dissection. It is reserved for stable Stanford B or follow-up imaging in chronic disease. (Source: Heart Foundation / CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

options.text: Commence intravenous labetalol infusion and admit to cardiology ward for observation
options.rationale: Beta-blockade and BP control are essential bridge therapy but not the definitive management for Stanford A. Type A requires emergency surgery; ward admission is inappropriate. (Source: Heart Foundation / CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

options.text: Proceed directly to operating theatre on the basis of the bedside TTE findings
options.rationale: Stanford type A dissection with confirmed intimal flap on TTE in the ascending aorta plus aortic regurgitation is a surgical emergency. Definitive imaging is helpful but not mandatory if the diagnosis is established and theatre is ready - delays for CT angiography in the setting of confirmed Stanford A worsens outcome. AU and international cardiothoracic practice supports proceeding to theatre with TOE in theatre to further characterise. Renal impairment is a secondary consideration; transoesophageal echocardiogram is the bedside alternative to CT in renal disease. (Source: Heart Foundation / CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

options.text: Insert intraaortic balloon pump as a bridge to definitive surgery now
options.rationale: IABP is contraindicated in aortic regurgitation and aortic dissection because it worsens both. (Source: Heart Foundation / CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

options.text: Proceed to CT angiography of the thoracic and abdominal aorta with iodinated contrast
options.rationale: CT angiography is the gold standard non-invasive imaging but iodinated contrast in eGFR 28 risks contrast-induced nephropathy, especially in stable haemodynamics; the TTE already shows the intimal flap and AR confirming Stanford A. Going to CT first delays surgery for marginal additional information. (Source: Heart Foundation / CSANZ ACS Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Stanford type A (ascending aorta) dissection is a surgical emergency with high mortality (1% per hour untreated); proceeding to theatre on bedside TTE with intimal flap and AR is appropriate when CT contrast risks AKI. Type B (descending aorta) is medically managed in uncomplicated cases. Bedside TOE is the contrast-free imaging alternative.
key_points: Stanford A = ascending aorta involvement, surgical emergency; Stanford B = descending only, medical management for uncomplicated
TTE with intimal flap and AR is sufficient bedside imaging to mobilise theatre when CT contrast risks AKI
Bilateral arm BP differential >20 mmHg with appropriate clinical features is a hallmark
explanation.pearls: Bridge therapy: titrate to systolic BP 100 to 120 mmHg with esmolol or labetalol first, then nicardipine if more BP control is needed (vasodilator without rate response is contraindicated alone). IABP is absolutely contraindicated.

subtopic_detail: Refractory hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state - insulin and fluid choice
stem:
An 84-year-old man with type 2 diabetes on metformin and gliclazide is brought in by his family after 5 days of decreased oral intake from a viral illness. He is now drowsy and oriented only to person. The ED registrar has resuscitated him with 2 L of 0.9% sodium chloride over 2 hours, and a maintenance insulin infusion at 0.05 unit/kg/hour was started 90 minutes ago. His glucose has fallen from 48 to 44 mmol/L. He still has minimal urine output (10 mL/hr).

His paired osmolality is being recalculated and the intensive care team is reviewing.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 118, BP 96/64 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 96% on 4 L/min, temperature 37.4 °C, weight 72 kg
data_table.Examination: Drowsy GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5), dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, no peritonism, no focal neurology, no diabetic foot infection
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 44 mmol/L (3.5-7.8), Na 152 mmol/L (135-145), corrected Na 162 mmol/L, K 4.6 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), bicarb 22 mmol/L, anion gap 12, ketones 0.4 mmol/L (<0.6), urea 22 mmol/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L (60-110), measured osmolality 348 mOsm/kg (275-295), lactate 2.6
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1g BD, gliclazide MR 60 mg daily; 2L 0.9% saline given; insulin infusion 0.05 unit/kg/hour running

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Double the insulin infusion to 0.1 unit/kg/hour to accelerate glucose decline
options.rationale: Doubling insulin risks dropping glucose too quickly with cerebral oedema risk; HHS is characterised by relative insulin sufficiency to suppress ketogenesis but inadequate for glycaemic control. The slower 0.05 unit/kg/hour rate is standard. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Stop the insulin infusion and continue saline-only until glucose below 30 mmol/L
options.rationale: Stopping insulin once the diagnosis is confirmed and resuscitation is underway will not adequately lower glucose. The insulin infusion is run alongside fluids, not in isolation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Switch to 5% dextrose / 0.9% sodium chloride and continue insulin infusion at current rate
options.rationale: Switching to dextrose before glucose has dropped below about 14 to 15 mmol/L is premature and would slow glucose correction excessively in someone still at 44. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Give intravenous sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol over 30 minutes for acidosis
options.rationale: There is no significant acidosis (HCO3 22, AG 12, lactate 2.6); bicarbonate is not indicated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Switch to 0.45% sodium chloride and continue insulin at 0.05 unit/kg/hour
options.rationale: HHS with corrected sodium above 150 mmol/L (this patient 162) and an effective osmolality of 348 requires a slow, controlled correction of both glucose and effective osmolality. Lowering osmolality faster than 3 to 8 mOsm/kg/hour and glucose by more than 4 to 5 mmol/L/hour risks cerebral oedema in elderly patients. Hypotonic fluids (0.45% sodium chloride) are switched to once initial volume restoration is achieved AND corrected sodium is rising. AU diabetes society guidelines support this slow correction; the insulin rate is intentionally low (0.05 unit/kg/hour) in HHS, not the 0.1 used in DKA. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: HHS is defined by severe hyperglycaemia (>30 mmol/L), effective osmolality >320 mOsm/kg, dehydration, altered mentation, and only minimal ketosis or acidosis. Fluid resuscitation is the dominant therapy. Switch to 0.45% sodium chloride only once intravascular volume is restored and corrected sodium is rising. Insulin is started low at 0.05 unit/kg/hour with target glucose drop of 4 to 5 mmol/L/hour to avoid cerebral oedema.
key_points: HHS osmolality drop should be no more than 3 to 8 mOsm/kg/hour to avoid cerebral oedema
Insulin rate in HHS is 0.05 unit/kg/hour (half that of DKA)
Switch to 0.45% sodium chloride after volume restoration AND if corrected sodium is rising; otherwise continue isotonic
explanation.pearls: Effective osmolality = 2 x Na + glucose (mmol/L). Mortality is high (5 to 20%), driven by underlying precipitants - sepsis, MI, stroke, drugs (steroids, atypicals). Anti-coagulation is indicated for prevention of arterial thrombosis given hyperviscosity.
